# suiban/vendor — TurboQuant patchset for the PrismML llama.cpp fork

This directory vendors the **TurboQuant KV-cache quantization patchset**: two new
GGML types, `TQ3_0` (3.5 bpw) and `TQ4_0` (4.5 bpw), applied on top of the pinned
PrismML fork release. CPU reference implementation (patches 0001–0004) plus a
clean-room CUDA backend (patches 0005–0007: KV-write quantize, dequantize rows,
flash-attention V dequant for the K=`q8_0`/`f16` × V=`TQ4_0`/`TQ3_0` pairs, and a
warp-shfl butterfly fast path for the fattn vec kernel); see
[`../docs/turboquant.md`](../docs/turboquant.md) for the algorithm and design
decisions, and [`../docs/benchmarks.md`](../docs/benchmarks.md) for measured
speed/quality numbers.

The fork checkout itself lives at `vendor/llama.cpp/` and is **never committed** —
it is cloned and patched at install time. (The repo `.gitignore` is intended to
exclude it; if `git status` ever shows `vendor/llama.cpp/`, do not add it.)

## Attribution & licensing

- **TQ3_0 is a port** of the MIT-licensed branch
  [`Aaryan-Kapoor/llama.cpp`, branch `turboquant-tq3_0`](https://github.com/Aaryan-Kapoor/llama.cpp),
  commit `1fb1fb3ab6b3e5c3776a362d7ac7f5985328d71b` — Copyright (c) Aaryan Kapoor,
  MIT License. The pipeline (RMS scale → golden-ratio sign flips + Walsh-Hadamard
  transform → Lloyd-Max lookup), the 8-level codebook constants, the 3-bit packing
  scheme, and the test tolerance (0.0060) come from that branch. Ported, not
  blind-applied: the PrismML base differs from the branch's upstream base.
- **TQ4_0 is our extension** of the same pattern to 16 levels (4-bit codes). The
  16-level codebook is generated by [`codebooks/generate.py`](codebooks/generate.py)
  and cross-checked against Max (1960).
- **Algorithm**: A. Zandieh, M. Daliri, M. Hagemann, S. Voinea, I. Han,
  *"TurboQuant: Online Vector Quantization with Near-optimal Distortion Rate"*,
  [arXiv:2504.19874](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19874).
- **Base code**: llama.cpp / ggml — Copyright (c) 2023-2026 The ggml authors,
  MIT License — as forked by PrismML (`PrismML-Eng/llama.cpp`, branch `prism`).
- **RHT-substitution consensus**: the randomized-Hadamard-transform stand-in for
  the paper's Gaussian rotation follows the llama.cpp community discussion
  [`ggml-org/llama.cpp#20969`](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions/20969)
  — cited for its ideas and reported numbers; no code from it is used.
- Everything authored here (patchset glue, TQ4_0 extension, tooling, docs) is
  Apache-2.0 like the rest of suiban; the ported TQ3_0 portions remain
  MIT-attributed as above. Both licenses are compatible with this use.
- The community CUDA gist (Jim Sullivan / veritatisquaesitoressumus) has **no
  license and none of its code is used here** — only its published measurement
  ideas are cited in the docs.

## Contents

| Path | What |
|---|---|
| `codebooks/generate.py` | stdlib-only Lloyd-Max codebook generator (deterministic) |
| `codebooks/tq_codebooks.h` | generated C header (checked in) — single source of truth for constants |
| `codebooks/tq_codebooks.json` | generated full-precision values, MSEs, cross-check results |
| `patches/0001-…core-types.patch` | ggml.h enum, block structs, reference quant/dequant, type traits |
| `patches/0002-…ggml-cpu….patch` | CPU backend: wrappers, `vec_dot` (vs q8_0), ops.cpp case lists |
| `patches/0003-…kv-cache-types.patch` | `common/arg.cpp`: accept `tq3_0`/`tq4_0` for `--cache-type-k/v` |
| `patches/0004-…tests-bench….patch` | test-quantize-fns tolerances, llama-bench type names |
| `patches/0005-…ggml-cuda….patch` | CUDA core: `turboquant.cuh` (device codebooks/quantize/dequant helpers), convert.cu dequantize rows (warp shfl-butterfly), set-rows.cu KV-write quantize, SET_ROWS supports_op |
| `patches/0006-…ggml-cuda-fattn….patch` | CUDA flash attention: `dequantize_V_tq3_0/tq4_0` + dispatch in fattn-common.cuh, kv-type gating in fattn.cu, 4 vec-kernel instance files (K∈{f16,q8_0} × V∈{tq3_0,tq4_0}, D 64/128/256), CMake wiring |
| `patches/0007-…warp-shfl-fast-path.patch` | CUDA flash attention: warp-cooperative shfl-butterfly fast path for the vec kernel's V dequant (the 0006 TODO); closed-form path kept as runtime fallback + HIP path |
| `apply_patches.py` | stdlib-only: `--clone` / apply (idempotent) / `--cpu-only` / `--cuda` builds |
| `run_kernel_tests.py` | stdlib-only: fork test suite + MSE/cosine + distribution robustness + head-dim rows + CUDA-algorithm equivalence (ctypes) + compiled-GPU stage |
| `tools/tq_cuda_numeric.cpp` | compiled-CUDA numeric validation (run_kernel_tests stage 7): dequant rows, SET_ROWS quantize, fattn V-dequant on-device vs the CPU reference |

Patched fork files — CPU patchset (12, mirroring the reference's 12-file
integration):
`common/arg.cpp`, `ggml/include/ggml.h`, `ggml/src/ggml-common.h`,
`ggml/src/ggml-quants.{c,h}`, `ggml/src/ggml.c`, `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/ggml-cpu.c`,
`ggml/src/ggml-cpu/quants.{c,h}`, `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/ops.cpp`,
`tests/test-quantize-fns.cpp`, `tools/llama-bench/llama-bench.cpp`.

CUDA patchset (10 more, all under `ggml/src/ggml-cuda/`): `turboquant.cuh`
(new), `convert.cu`, `set-rows.cu`, `ggml-cuda.cu`, `fattn-common.cuh`
(patches 0006 + 0007), `fattn.cu`, `fattn-vec.cuh`, `CMakeLists.txt`,
`template-instances/generate_cu_files.py`, plus 4 new
`template-instances/fattn-vec-instance-{f16,q8_0}-{tq3_0,tq4_0}.cu`.

## Type IDs (verified against the fork, not assumed)

The fork's enum tail at tag `prism-b9596-9fcaed7` is
`GGML_TYPE_Q1_0 = 41`, `GGML_TYPE_Q2_0 = 42`, `GGML_TYPE_COUNT = 43`.
The patchset appends:

```
GGML_TYPE_TQ3_0 = 43   // 14 B / 32 values = 3.5 bpw (fp16 RMS scale + 12 B of 3-bit codes)
GGML_TYPE_TQ4_0 = 44   // 18 B / 32 values = 4.5 bpw (fp16 RMS scale + 16 B of 4-bit codes)
GGML_TYPE_COUNT = 45
```

These IDs are only meaningful inside our patched fork build. Mainline's
`TQ1_0`/`TQ2_0` (ids 34/35) are TriLM *ternary weight* formats — unrelated to
TurboQuant despite the similar names.

## Apply / build / test

```sh
cd suiban/vendor
python3 apply_patches.py --clone --cpu-only   # shallow-clone pinned tag, patch, build (CPU)
python3 run_kernel_tests.py                   # stages 1-6 (CPU); stage 7 runs when build-cuda + a GPU exist
python3 apply_patches.py --cuda               # CUDA backend build (needs the CUDA toolkit)
python3 run_kernel_tests.py --require-cuda    # same, but a skipped stage 7 is a failure
```

Pinned base: `https://github.com/PrismML-Eng/llama.cpp` tag `prism-b9596-9fcaed7`.
Builds are `Release`, shared libs, targets `test-quantize-fns` and `llama-bench`
only (they cover every patched file; the CUDA build compiles the whole
`ggml-cuda` backend as their dependency). Re-runs are idempotent:
already-applied patches are skipped, builds are incremental. CPU build:
`GGML_CUDA=OFF` in `build-cpu/`. CUDA build (`--cuda`): `GGML_CUDA=ON`
`GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON` in `build-cuda/`; nvcc is looked up on PATH, then
`CUDA_HOME`/`CUDA_PATH`, then `/usr/local/cuda`, and a missing toolkit is an
explained exit 2 (a GPU driver alone cannot compile the kernels —
`suiban install turboquant` surfaces this and stays on the fallback chain).

`suiban install turboquant` drives the same script as
`apply_patches.py --clone --backend=cpu|cuda` (an alias for
`--cpu-only`/`--cuda`); its `--install` step additionally builds and promotes
`llama-server` and `llama-perplexity` (the latter powers `suiban bench kv`'s
PPL column) plus the fork's shared libs into `~/.bonsai/bin/<backend>/`.

If the system C++ compiler is newer than the CUDA toolkit supports (e.g. GCC 16
with CUDA 13.x, which supports GCC ≤ 15), pass a compatible host compiler
(or set the standard CMake env var `CUDAHOSTCXX`):

```sh
python3 apply_patches.py --cuda --cuda-host-compiler /path/to/g++-15
```

## Measured results (CPU reference, x86-64, this patchset)

`tests/test-quantize-fns` (synthetic cosine data, test size 4096):

| type | max quant error (tolerance) | reference error | dot product error (tolerance) |
|---|---|---|---|
| tq3_0 | **0.004182** (0.0060, from the MIT reference) | 0.000000 | 0.061406 (0.15) |
| tq4_0 | **0.002111** (0.0030, set empirically — target was ≤0.0060) | 0.000000 | 0.017466 (0.15) |

Standalone harness (`run_kernel_tests.py` stage 3, 100 000 random N(0,1)
32-blocks, seed 20260721), vs the paper's MSE targets (b=3: 0.03 theory /
0.034 community; b=4: 0.009 / 0.0093):

| type | relative MSE (target) | round-trip cosine similarity |
|---|---|---|
| tq3_0 | **0.032450** (≤ 0.0345) | 0.983655 |
| tq4_0 | **0.008921** (≤ 0.0096) | 0.995532 |

Stage 4 stresses the white-noise assumption (10 seeds × 4 000 blocks per case,
worst case asserted against measured envelopes — see the comments at
`ROBUST_MSE_ENVELOPES` for the honest analysis):

| input distribution | tq3_0 worst rel. MSE | tq4_0 worst rel. MSE | honest reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| Student-t, df=3 (heavy tails) | 0.031407 | 0.008595 | no degradation — the RMS scale + rotation absorb tails |
| ×100 spikes on 2 of 32 channels | 0.020603 | 0.008869 | TOTAL MSE fine (outlier energy quantizes well), **but** the non-outlier channels reconstruct at 4.3–5.0× (tq3) / 2.9–3.0× (tq4) their own energy — worse than zeroing. Real per-channel-outlier cost of a block-RMS format; asserted as a band, not hidden |

Stage 5 quantizes row-shaped tensors at head dims 64/96/128/256 (multi-block
rows): byte layout identical to flat quantization, Gaussian MSE targets hold at
every row length.

Stage 6 validates the **CUDA dequant algorithm** host-side on every case above:
the CUDA kernels invert the rotation with a closed-form parity sum
(`y[j] = d/√32 · sign[j] · Σ_k (−1)^popc(j&k) · C[idx_k]`) rather than the CPU's
sequential butterfly; the harness re-implements that exact math (including the
bit-unpack helpers and the packed sign mask `0x696B4B4A`) on real C-quantized
bytes and compares against the C dequantizer: max |model − C| is 5.8e-07
(tq3_0) / 5.2e-07 (tq4_0) on white Gaussian input, 3.3e-05 / 4.7e-05 on the
×100-outlier blocks (tolerance scales with the block scale `d` ≈ 100), and
≤ 5.2e-07 across all head-dim row cases.

## Measured results (compiled CUDA kernels, ON the GPU)

`run_kernel_tests.py` stage 7 compiles [`tools/tq_cuda_numeric.cpp`](tools/tq_cuda_numeric.cpp)
against the `build-cuda` libs and runs the actual kernels on-device against the
CPU reference. RTX 3070 Ti Laptop (8 GB, sm_86), CUDA 13.2, patches 0001–0007
applied — measured max deviations:

| kernel surface | tq3_0 | tq4_0 | assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| dequantize rows, contiguous f32 (131 072 values) | 4.768e-07 | 2.384e-07 | ≤ 1e-4 |
| dequantize rows, contiguous f16 (vs f32 reference) | 1.520e-03 | 1.355e-03 | ≤ 2e-3 (fp16 rounding) |
| dequantize rows, strided nc (fattn prefill layout) | 2.384e-07 | 2.384e-07 | ≤ 1e-4 |
| SET_ROWS KV-write quantize (byte mismatch / dequant dev) | 0 / 0.0 | 0 / 0.0 | ≤ 1% / ≤ 0.2 |
| FLASH_ATTN_EXT vec path (nb=1, D 64/128/256, NMSE) | ≤ 2.6e-08 | ≤ 2.5e-08 | ≤ 5e-4 (fork's own threshold) |
| FLASH_ATTN_EXT prefill path (nb=512, NMSE) | ≤ 3.3e-05 | ≤ 3.3e-05 | ≤ 5e-4 |

SET_ROWS came out bit-identical to the CPU quantizer on this GPU; that is
observed, not guaranteed (`-use_fast_math` may still flip codes at Lloyd-Max
boundaries on other hardware — the assertion allows ≤ 1% mismatched bytes and
one level step of reconstruction deviation).

## CUDA build validation status

- **Compiles**: yes — CUDA toolkit 13.2 (nvcc V13.2.86), native arch sm_86,
  host compiler g++ 15 (the system GCC 16 is newer than CUDA 13.2 supports —
  hence `--cuda-host-compiler`), both build flavors' TurboQuant surface (the TQ
  vec instances and all patched files compile identically with and without
  `GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS`).
- **Runs on a GPU**: yes — validated on an RTX 3070 Ti Laptop (8 GB):
  - compiled-kernel numeric runs vs the CPU reference (stage 7 table above);
  - `llama-server --cache-type-v tq4_0`/`tq3_0` end-to-end with the 1-bit
    Bonsai 27B: needle retrieval passed at 10/50/90 % depths and
    `llama-perplexity` matched the q8_0 baseline within error bars
    (integration pass 2026-07-21, re-run with the full `suiban bench kv`
    battery — see `../docs/turboquant.md` and `../docs/benchmarks.md`);
  - `llama-bench` speed tables before/after the 0007 fast path in
    [`../docs/benchmarks.md`](../docs/benchmarks.md).
- **Not validated**: HIP/ROCm (falls back to the closed-form path by
  construction, never compiled or run here), Metal (no kernels exist —
  `TODO(v1.1)`).

Honest note on cosine similarity: the community gist reported ~0.997 for 4-bit on
real KV tensors. On synthetic white Gaussian data the information-theoretic
expectation is ≈ √(1−MSE) ≈ 0.9952, which is exactly what we measure — real KV
activations are more structured and quantize better than white noise. We report
the synthetic number because it is reproducible.

## Honest notes / known divergences

- **Rotation substitution**: the paper's rotation is a QR-decomposed Gaussian
  matrix; this implementation (following the MIT branch and community consensus)
  uses a randomized Hadamard transform (deterministic golden-ratio sign flips +
  5-stage WHT, 1/√32-normalized). Documented in `../docs/turboquant.md`.
- **All-zero blocks**: the MIT reference stored scale 1.0 for vanishing blocks,
  which dequantizes to centroid noise. We store `d = 0` so all-zero blocks
  round-trip to exact zeros. This is the only behavioral divergence from the
  reference kernel.
- **Scalar only (CPU)**: `vec_dot` is a scalar reference (dequant through
  inverse RHT, then dot with q8_0). No SIMD/arch-specific variants in v1 —
  correctness first.
  `TODO(v1.1): AVX2 path for the WHT butterfly if profiling shows it matters.`
- **CUDA (patches 0005–0007) — clean-room**, designed from the CPU reference
  semantics and mainline's quantized-KV CUDA idioms only; nothing from the
  unlicensed community gist. Notes:
  - The task-relevant fork has no `ggml_cuda_fattn_kv_type_supported` function;
    the equivalent gating lives in `ggml_cuda_get_best_fattn_kernel`
    (`fattn.cu`) and that is where the pair rules were patched.
  - **Mixed-pair choice (documented decision)**: the fork's dispatch makes both
    options workable, so we did both. The 4 TQ vec instances compile in every
    build flavor and the K=`f16`/`q8_0` × V=`tq3_0`/`tq4_0` pairs are
    explicitly allowed in the support check — `GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS` is
    **not required** for suiban's pairs. `apply_patches.py --cuda` still turns
    the flag ON so every other mixed K/V combination a user might configure
    also gets kernels. A TurboQuant V with any *other* K type returns
    "unsupported" (never reaches the vec dispatch abort): the op falls back to
    the CPU backend, which has full TQ support — degraded, never crashed.
  - fattn's per-thread `dequantize_V` contract cannot see the whole block —
    the price of a rotation-based format. Patch 0006 shipped a closed-form
    parity-sum reference (~32 adds per output element); the decode-bound
    long-context case did show up (measured: tg128 at 16K depth dropped to
    ~6.5 t/s vs ~36 t/s for V=q8_0 on the 8 GB tier), so **patch 0007 adds the
    warp-cooperative shfl-butterfly fast path**: the vec kernel hands the 8
    four-code groups of a block to 8 consecutive lanes, and the inverse WHT
    runs as 2 in-register + 3 `__shfl_xor_sync` stages. The lane-pattern
    assumption is verified warp-wide at runtime (`__all_sync`), so any other
    caller — and HIP, where the fast path is compiled out — degrades to the
    closed-form path instead of hitting shuffle UB. Before/after numbers:
    [`../docs/benchmarks.md`](../docs/benchmarks.md).
  - The KV-write path is `GGML_OP_SET_ROWS` (what this fork's KV cache uses on
    CUDA). `GGML_OP_CPY` f32↔TQ and the bf16 convert paths are intentionally
    not wired: nothing in the fork's KV graphs needs them, and unsupported ops
    fall back to the CPU backend. Quantized codes are produced by the same
    algorithm as the CPU kernel but are not guaranteed bit-identical to CPU
    output (nvcc `-use_fast_math` may reassociate float ops near centroid
    boundaries); the storage layout is byte-identical and either side can read
    the other's blocks.
  - Runtime GPU correctness is **validated**: `run_kernel_tests.py` stage 7
    runs the compiled kernels on-device against the CPU reference
    (test-backend-ops-style graph comparisons for SET_ROWS and
    FLASH_ATTN_EXT, direct calls for the convert kernels — measured table
    above), on top of the host-side algorithm-equivalence harness (stage 6).
- **End-to-end V-cache use requires flash attention**. The CPU FA path reads V
  via `ggml_get_type_traits(v->type)->to_float` and writes the cache via generic
  `SET_ROWS`/`from_float`, so no extra CPU wiring was needed. Live
  `llama-server --cache-type-v tq4_0`/`tq3_0` was validated for real on the
  8 GB CUDA tier (integration pass + the `suiban bench kv` battery: needle,
  replay and `llama-perplexity` deltas — see `../docs/turboquant.md`).
- **Codebook sync rule**: the constants in `patches/0001` (CPU, ggml-quants.c)
  and `patches/0005` (CUDA, turboquant.cuh) are copied from
  `codebooks/tq_codebooks.h`. If you re-run the generator and values change,
  regenerate BOTH patches — a checked-in consistency test is in the suiban test
  suite's remit, not here (vendor tooling stays stdlib-only and standalone).
  `run_kernel_tests.py` stage 6 reads its centroids from
  `codebooks/tq_codebooks.json`, so a silent CPU/CUDA constant drift would fail
  that stage.
- Patches are exact-context diffs against the pinned tag. Bumping the fork
  release requires re-porting (apply, fix contexts, re-diff) — `apply_patches.py`
  fails loudly if the clone is at any other tag.
